main_summary.summary: Johnnie Ruth Brawner McCullar (b. February 4, 1940, Sasser, Terrell County, Georgia) recounts her path from a one-room rural schoolhouse to the SNCC-supported Terrell County voter-registration movement of 1962. Interviewed March 9, 2013, on the Albany State University campus by Dr. Hasan Kwame Jeffries with videographer Petna Dalico, McCullar describes growing up on a Worth County family farm before her family moved to Sasser, attending Piney Grove, Dunbar, and Carver High School in Dawson under Jim Crow conditions: torn-page hand-me-down textbooks, partitioned classrooms, no lunchroom. She enrolled at Albany State in 1960, participated in her first march downtown alongside Bertha Gober, and returned home in 1962 after hepatitis from cafeteria food forced her out of school. Back in Terrell County, she became secretary of the Terrell County Movement under Lucius Holloway, attended mass meetings at Mount Olive Baptist Church, registered as a voter (her mother lost her cafeteria job for doing the same), canvassed door-to-door, hosted SNCC field workers Charles Sherrod, Prathia Hall, and Ralph Allen, and survived Sheriff Z.T. Mathews's intimidation visits. After Mount Olive was burned in August 1962, she helped continue meetings in a tent behind the foundation. She later worked as a Certified Nursing Assistant at Phoebe Putney Memorial Hospital in Albany and served as an NAACP deputy registrar from 1979 to 1996.
key_themes: Albany State and the Albany Movement
main_summary.historical_significance:
McCullar's testimony documents the day-to-day operation of the Terrell County Movement from a member-level vantage point rarely preserved in the canonical Albany/SNCC narrative. As secretary of the local organization under Lucius Holloway, she kept the meeting notes that the U.S. Department of Justice later confiscated as evidence — establishing her recollections as both primary source and historical-record gap (her notebook never returned). Her account names the Sheriff Z.T. Mathews intimidation pattern at Mount Olive mass meetings, the Reverend Samuel Wells-led Dawson restaurant sit-ins, the church-burning sequence of August 1962, the family-vigil response to retaliatory-arson threats, and the GBI-versus-DOJ contrast in the federal response.

The interview also captures the economic reprisal pattern: her mother's dismissal from the white school's cafeteria after registering to vote, the white Albany employer who refused to honor the firing request, and McCullar's own withdrawal from Albany State after hepatitis. Her later work as a deputy registrar from 1979 through 1996 documents the long arc from movement-era voter mobilization to institutionalized registration access.

chapters.title: Family Origins in Terrell County
chapters.summary: McCullar identifies herself as born February 4, 1940, in Sasser, Terrell County, Georgia, to Clifford Brawner and Johnnie Lou Brawner. Her father's family came from Worth County; her mother's family originated in Alabama. She describes her mother as constantly working — canning, sewing, making quilts — and her father as a farmer who later worked at the Marine Base and then for 28 years at the Swift fertilizer plant in Albany.
related_events: Family migration from Worth to Terrell County

chapters.title: Farm Childhood and Move to Sasser
chapters.summary: McCullar describes the family farm near Doublegate on Old Dawson Road and her dislike of farm life — the animals, heat, mosquitoes, gnats, and snakes. She recounts feeding chickens, milking the cow, and picking cotton. The family moved off the farm in the 1950s to Sasser when she was about 12 or 13. She preferred Sasser primarily because there were fewer snakes.
related_events: Brawner family relocation from farm to Sasser

chapters.title: Segregated Schooling
chapters.summary: McCullar attended Piney Grove, a one-room school in Terrell County with about 40 students across seven grades under teacher Vanny Wakefield. She moved to Dunbar Elementary in Sasser through ninth grade, then to Carver High School in Dawson. She describes the unheated classrooms, the lack of a lunchroom, the partitioned auditorium, and the hand-me-down textbooks with torn pages — one spelling book per class — passed down from white schools.

chapters.title: Awareness Without Discussion
chapters.summary: McCullar reflects that Brown v. Board was not discussed in school or at home — black teachers were 'told what to teach,' and her parents 'didn't know too much about segregation.' But the inequality was visible: torn pages, shared books, the dust-compound classroom-cleaning duty that fell on the students. She graduated in 1959 and sat out a year babysitting and keeping house while her older sister finished nursing school in Bibb County, Macon, Georgia.

chapters.title: Albany State and the First March
chapters.summary: McCullar enrolled at Albany State in 1960, choosing it because her parents wanted her close given her childhood illness. She declared a science major and lived on campus. The administration ignored complaints about the food; she contracted hepatitis from cafeteria meals. In the fall of 1961, she participated in her first march downtown — recalling Bertha Gober as the only name she retained from that day. Her parents accepted her involvement after a white head cook at her mother's school made disapproving remarks.

chapters.title: Joining the Terrell County Movement
chapters.summary: Forced home by illness in spring 1962, McCullar found her parents already attending mass meetings — first at Zion Hill Sardis Baptist Church in Dawson, then at the Brawner family home. She became secretary of the Terrell County Movement under president Lucius Holloway. The organization had begun in 1960 but she joined upon returning. She kept notes of mass meetings where SNCC field workers Charles Sherrod, Don Harris, Prathia Hall, and Ralph Allen spoke.

chapters.title: Voter Registration and Reprisal
chapters.summary: McCullar describes attempts to register at the Terrell County courthouse, where the registrar would leave the office to avoid prospective Black registrants. When the registrar was present, applicants had to read scripture from the Bible before completing the form. After McCullar and her mother successfully registered, her mother was fired from her cooking job at the white school. The Albany employer who later hired her mother refused a call asking her to fire her, saying her registration was not her concern.

chapters.title: SNCC Field Workers and Sheriff Mathews
chapters.summary: McCullar describes the SNCC field workers' regular presence in Terrell County — Charles Sherrod, Don Harris, Prathia Hall, Ralph Allen — many staying at Carolyn Daniels's house and at Lucius Holloway's. Mount Olive Baptist Church hosted the Wednesday-night Terrell mass meetings, with the surrounding SW Georgia counties rotating support. Sheriff Z.T. Mathews and his deputies would enter the meetings, put their feet on the pews, and verbally harass attendees while the singing or speaking continued.

chapters.title: Canvassing and Restaurant Sit-Ins
chapters.summary: McCullar canvassed Dawson neighborhoods after school with Gladys and Annadine Clark and other students, walking door-to-door despite locals running back into their houses calling them 'Freedom Riders.' Most refused to talk for fear of losing jobs or housing. Reverend Samuel Wells led restaurant sit-ins at a Dawson establishment — sitting at the counter, leaving when asked. McCullar describes the workers and owners cursing them and the practice of getting hamburgers through a hole-in-the-wall window because Black patrons could not enter.

chapters.title: Mount Olive Burned, Family Vigils
chapters.summary: Mount Olive Baptist Church was burned in August 1962. McCullar describes the loss 'like a death.' The mass meetings moved temporarily to a Methodist church, then to a tent erected behind the rebuilding foundation when the Methodist congregation feared their own church would be burned next. The Brawner family began taking nightly turns watching the house for strange cars or arson attempts, with a plan to evacuate everyone before fire could be set.

chapters.title: Federal Court and Awakening
chapters.summary: After the church burning, the Georgia Bureau of Investigation came first — in McCullar's view, to cover for the perpetrators — followed by the U.S. Department of Justice, which took statements and confiscated McCullar's secretary's notebook to Washington (never returned). Sasser residents traveled to federal court in Americus regarding shootings at SNCC workers and the church burnings. McCullar reflects that working with the movement first opened her eyes to the depth of Jim Crow — the hole-in-the-wall hamburger window, the closed restaurants — that her parents had previously protected her from.

chapters.title: Marriage, Return, and Continued Service
chapters.summary: McCullar married in 1967 and moved to Topeka, Kansas, then Atlanta for four years, then back to Albany to work as a Certified Nursing Assistant at Phoebe Putney Memorial Hospital. She found Terrell transformed — schools integrated, segregated water fountains gone. She resumed activism with the NAACP, became a deputy registrar in 1979 and served through 1996, registering voters four hours each Saturday morning. She names JL Barnes as the Terrell County NAACP branch president who was murdered in 1994.
related_events: Voter registration expansion under Voting Rights Act

chapters.title: Closing Reflections
chapters.summary: McCullar concludes that progress is real but incomplete — 'everything is not coming up roses, and I don't think it ever will be.' She points to the obstruction faced by the Black president of her era as evidence that opposition to Black political power persists. Her closing charge is for Black voters to register, vote, and hold elected officials accountable.

loc_video.caption: Johnnie Ruth McCullar oral history interview conducted by Hasan Kwame Jeffries in Albany, Georgia, 2013 March 09
